15: A direct revelation: 'The saying is trustworthy and deserving of full acceptance, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the foremost.' The first of the Pastoral Epistles' five 'faithful sayings' (pistos ho logos) is a Christological mission-statement: the incarnation's purpose is soteriological — Christ came into the world (implying pre-existence, like John 1:14) specifically to save sinners. Paul's self-designation as 'the foremost of sinners' makes the statement an autobiographical proof: if Christ saved me (the persecutor of the church), his saving purpose is as wide as the worst sinner.
1: Paul's apostleship is 'by the command of God our Savior and of Christ Jesus our hope' — the authority behind the entire letter is Christological. The dual title 'God our Savior / Christ Jesus our hope' places Father and Son as co-sources of the apostolic mandate. Christ as 'our hope' (tēs elpidos hēmōn) concentrates the entire eschatological expectation in his person: not a doctrine of hope but a person who is hope — the risen, returning Lord in whom the future is secured.
2: Timothy as 'my true son in the faith' (gnēsiō teknō en pistei) — the faith that binds Paul and Timothy as father and son is faith in Christ. The fatherhood is spiritual, mediated through the gospel and the Christ who is its content. The triad 'grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord' places Christ as co-giver of the divine blessings: grace is the undeserved favor enacted at the cross; mercy is the ongoing compassion that follows from it; peace is the reconciliation between God and humanity accomplished by Christ.
3: The instruction to remain in Ephesus to combat false teaching is Christologically motivated — the false teachers are corrupting 'the stewardship (oikonomia) of God that is by faith.' The management of God's household is the administration of the gospel of Christ; the false teachers' speculative genealogies undermine the Christ-centered simplicity of that gospel. Timothy's pastoral mandate is fundamentally a Christological one: to guard the proclamation of Christ against distorting substitutes.
4: Myths and genealogies 'promote speculations rather than the stewardship of God that is by faith' — the counter to speculative theological elaboration is the Christ-centered oikonomia. The household of God (1 Timothy 3:15) is organized around the gospel; the administrator of that household (Timothy) manages it by maintaining the Christological center of its proclamation. The false teaching's fundamental error is Christological displacement: something other than Christ becomes the organizing center of religious inquiry.
5: 'The goal of this instruction is love from a pure heart, good conscience, and sincere faith' — the triad describes the Christological character formation that sound teaching produces. Love is the identifying mark of the disciples (John 13:35); the pure heart is what the beatitudes promise to those who see God (Matthew 5:8); the sincere faith is the trusting relationship with Christ. The goal of Christian instruction is not doctrinal expertise but Christomorphic character — being shaped by Christ's own love, purity, and faithfulness.
6: Departure from love, good conscience, and sincere faith into 'meaningless talk' — the Christological failure behind vain theological dispute is a loss of the personal: when Christ is no longer the center of devotion and inquiry, the center is filled by speculation. The false teachers have drifted from a person (Christ) to a system of ideas. Christological reality is relational before it is propositional; the departure Paul diagnoses is first a departure from relationship.
7: 'Wanting to be teachers of the law without understanding what they say or what they affirm' — the false teachers seek the authority of Torah-interpretation without recognizing that Christ is the telos of the law (Romans 10:4). The law rightly used reveals sin and drives to Christ (Galatians 3:24); misused, it becomes an autonomous system of merit or speculation. The teachers of the law who do not understand the law's Christological function are the NT counterpart of Jesus's opponents in the Gospels.
8: 'The law is good if one uses it properly' — the Christological key to proper law-use is Paul's own argument in Romans: the law diagnoses sin, but Christ is the remedy (Romans 3:20-22; 1 Timothy 7:12-25; 1 Timothy 8:1-4). The law properly used serves the gospel: it exposes what Christ came to cure. The false teachers use the law improperly — not as a diagnostic leading to Christ but as a constructive religious system that replaces him.
9: The law is made 'not for the righteous but for lawbreakers' — in Christ, the believer has been declared righteous (Romans 5:1; 1 Timothy 8:1); the law's condemning function no longer applies to those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1: there is therefore now no condemnation). The vice list exposes the unredeemed condition that Christ came to address. The law's portrait of sin is simultaneously the portrait of what Christ saves from — the scope of his salvation matches the scope of the transgression the law identifies.
10: The vice list culminates with 'whatever else is contrary to sound doctrine' — sound doctrine is literally 'healthy teaching' (didaskaliai hygianousēs), a medical metaphor: the gospel is the medicine that heals the disease of sin catalogued in 1 Timothy 1:9-10. Christ as the physician who heals sin's disease is the underlying Christological metaphor (Luke 5:31-32: the well have no need of a physician; I came not to call the righteous but sinners to repentance).
11: 'The gospel of the glory of the blessed God, which he entrusted to me' — the gospel is the gospel of God's glory: it reveals what God is truly like. Christ is the one in whose face the knowledge of the glory of God shines (2 Corinthians 4:6); the gospel that Paul proclaims is the proclamation of this Christ-glory. The entrustment (episteutheēn) to Paul is a commission of stewardship: he manages the Christ-glory proclamation on God's behalf.
12: 'I thank Christ Jesus our Lord, who gave me strength' — the thanksgiving is to Christ personally, not abstractly to God. Christ is the direct agent of Paul's empowerment (endunamōsanti) — the same verb as Ephesians 6:10 (be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might) and Philippians 4:13 (I can do all things through him who strengthens me). Paul's entire apostolic capacity is Christological: he works because Christ works in him.
13: 'I was shown mercy because I acted ignorantly in unbelief' — Christ's mercy toward the persecutor Paul is the paradigmatic demonstration of the gospel's power. The one who called the church a blasphemy (by persecuting it) is transformed into its foremost apostle. The Christological significance: Christ's mercy is not limited by the severity of the offense but extends to the worst imaginable case — the violent opponent of Christ himself. This is the mercy that defines the gospel.
14: 'The grace of our Lord overflowed for me with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus' — grace, faith, and love are all 'in Christ Jesus': they exist within the sphere of union with him. The grammar is significant: not faith and love directed toward Christ (though that is true) but faith and love that are located in him, participating in what he is. Christ's own faithfulness (pistis Christou) and his own love become the believer's through incorporation into him.
16: 'That in me, the foremost sinner, Christ Jesus might display his perfect patience as an example to those who were to believe in him for eternal life' — Paul is the hypotypōsis (pattern, prototype) of Christ's patience. The display is Christocentric: Christ's patience (makrothymia) is what is displayed, not Paul's virtue. Christ's willingness to pursue, call, and transform the most violent opponent of the church demonstrates the limitless extension of his saving patience. Every subsequent conversion is implicit in Paul's — if Christ was patient enough for the chief of sinners, he is patient enough for any.
17: The doxology — 'to the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory for ever and ever' — is addressed to the God who is the source of the grace Paul has just narrated. The Christological movement: the doxology to God the Father rises from the Christological narrative of 1 Timothy 1:12-16. God is praised for what he has done in Christ; the doxology is the response to the gospel, not an abstract theistic affirmation.
18: 'According to the prophecies previously made about you, that by them you may wage the good warfare' — Timothy's ministry is Christological warfare: the 'good warfare' (tēn kalēn strateian) is the apostolic mission of proclaiming Christ against the opposition of false teaching and the powers behind it (Ephesians 6:10-18). The prophetic commissionings that equipped Timothy for ministry are the Spirit's preparation for Christological service; the warfare is the cosmic conflict between the gospel and its opponents.
19: 'Holding faith and a good conscience' — faith in Christ and the moral consciousness that corresponds to that faith go together; abandoning conscience ruptures the faith. The nautical metaphor of shipwreck — the most catastrophic possible failure — underlines the seriousness of moral-spiritual integrity. The faith that is shipwrecked is specifically faith in Christ; the conscience that is abandoned is the internal witness to Christ's moral standard in the believer's life.
20: 'Handed over to Satan that they may learn not to blaspheme' — the disciplinary handing-over has a corrective goal: the blasphemer may learn not to blaspheme. Even in judgment, the Christological mercy operates: the purpose is not destruction but the recovery of those who have spoken against Christ's gospel. The formula mirrors 1 Corinthians 5:5 where the same discipline aims at the salvation of the spirit 'in the day of the Lord Jesus' — even the most severe church discipline is eschatologically oriented toward Christ's final judgment as the criterion.
